elements.sent-2411-002: To safeguard sensitive user data, web developers typically rely on an implicit access-control policies, which they implement using access checks and query filters.
elements.sent-2411-003: This ad-hoc approach is error-prone, as these scattered checks and filters are easy to misplace or misspecify; and the lack of an explicit policy precludes external access-control enforcement.
elements.sent-2411-004: More critically, it is difficult to divine what policy is embedded in application code and what data the application may access--- an issue that worsens as development teams evolve.
elements.sent-2411-005: This paper tackles policy extraction: the task of extracting the access-control policy embedded in an application by summarizing its data queries.
elements.sent-2411-006: An extracted policy, once vetted for errors, can stand alone as a specification for the application's data access, and can be enforced to ensure compliance as code changes over time.
elements.sent-2411-007: We introduce \name, a policy extractor for Ruby-on-Rails web applications.
elements.sent-2411-008: It then merges and simplifies these traces into a final policy that aligns with the observed behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-009: We applied \name to three real-world applications and compare extracted policies to handwritten ones, revealing several errors in the latter.
elements.sent-2411-010: Protecting sensitive data from unauthorized access is a critical concern for today's web applications.
elements.sent-2411-011: Therefore, when building web applications, developers must determine what access-control policy the application should enact--- for example, a university might want a policy that ensures a student's grades are visible only to the student and their instructors.
elements.sent-2411-012: In today's applications, access-control policies are embedded in application code.
elements.sent-2411-013: Furthermore, in most cases they are spread across several functions and in the filter predicates of multiple database queries.
elements.sent-2411-014: This practice is error-prone---missing or misspecified access checks have previously led to sensitive-data exposure~\cite{stock18:hotcrp_hidden_tags,kohler13:hotcrp_hide_rounds,kohler15:hotcrp_download_pc,ashford15:facebook_photo,maunder16:wordpress,green17:piazza .}
elements.sent-2411-015: But more fundamentally, because the policy is never explicitly stated, it is difficult for anyone other than the application's developer to understand what policy is embedded in the code.
elements.sent-2411-016: Worse, as time passes, even the application development team is unlikely to remember the policy, and is unlikely to be able to reconstruct it from application code.
elements.sent-2411-017: While there have been research frameworks that require explicit policy specification~\cite{DBLP:conf/popl/YangYS12,DBLP:conf/pldi/YangHASFC16,DBLP:conf/pldi/AustinYFS13 , we aim to address legacy applications rather than requiring them to be rewritten in such frameworks.}
elements.sent-2411-018: In this paper, we tackle the task of policy extraction: Given a web application, extracting its implicitly-embedded access-control policy by summarizing its possible data accesses.
elements.sent-2411-019: A human can then review the extracted policy to better understand the application's data accesses, and to ensure they are within the bounds of intended data revelations.
elements.sent-2411-020: If this is not the case, the application likely has an access-check bug, which can then be fixed.
elements.sent-2411-021: Once reviewed, the policy can be enforced using an external access-control mechanism~\cite{DBLP:conf/uss/MehtaEH0D17,DBLP:conf/hotos/MarzoevASYKMKM19,DBLP:conf/osdi/ZhangSCPSS22 to ensure the application's continued compliance.}
elements.sent-2411-022: We present an approach for extracting access-control policies from legacy web applications.
elements.sent-2411-023: It first explores execution paths through application code using concolic execution~\cite{DBLP:conf/pldi/GodefroidKS05,DBLP:conf/sigsoft/SenMA05 , producing transcripts that record the conditions under which each SQL query is issued~(\Cref{sec:concolic ).}}
elements.sent-2411-024: It then merges and simplifies these transcripts to produce a policy, expressed as view definitions~\cite{DBLP:conf/icde/Motro89,DBLP:conf/dmdw/RosenthalS00,DBLP:conf/sigmod/RizviMSR04 , that allows each recorded query to be issued under its conditions~(\Cref{sec:policy ).}}
elements.sent-2411-025: A key challenge of this approach is scalability: Web code is often complex with many branches, resulting in a large number of paths that makes exhaustive exploration infeasible.
elements.sent-2411-026: Luckily, while an application as a whole might be complex, we empirically observe that the logic governing SQL query issuance often consists of only a few simple types of control- and data-flow operations~(\Cref{sec:concolic:simple ).}
elements.sent-2411-027: We thus tailor concolic execution to track only those operations, reducing the number of branches to explore as well as our implementation effort.
elements.sent-2411-028: We implemented this approach in \name, a policy-extraction tool for web applications written in Ruby on Rails.
elements.sent-2411-029: We then applied \name to three real-world applications, two of which we had previously written policies for by hand.
elements.sent-2411-030: When we compared the extracted policies to the handwritten ones~(\Cref{sec:eval:compare ), we identified several formerly-unknown errors in the latter--- including several overly-permissive views can reveal sensitive data to unauthorized users.}
elements.sent-2411-031: This underscores the difficulty of understanding access-control logic in a complex legacy application, and testifies to the utility of policy extraction in aiding this understanding.
elements.sent-2411-032: A further review of the extracted policies also uncovered a subtle bug we had inadvertently introduced into application code that silently removed an access check.
elements.sent-2411-033: In terms of limitations: Due to its reliance of concolic execution, \name cannot guarantee that the extracted policy covers all possible application queries or captures every condition under which the queries are issued~(\Cref{sec:overview:scope ), and it does not scale to all web handlers~(\Cref{sec:eval:perf ).}}
elements.sent-2411-034: In addition, \name relies on the user to specify input constraints and provide hints to prune the exploration space~(\Cref{sec:overview:workflow ), supports only a subset of SQL~(\Cref{sec:policy:simplify ), and could use a better user interface for policy auditing.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Nevertheless, we were encouraged by the usefulness of the policies \name has already managed to extract, and see this as a meaningful step towards better access control for web applications.
elements.sent-2411-036: We were prompted to tackle the policy-extraction problem by our earlier experience hand-crafting policies for existing web applications.
elements.sent-2411-037: A few years ago, while investigating externally-enforced access control for web applications, \footnote{By ``externally-enforced'', we mean having an enforcer program mediate the application's database queries, preventing accesses deemed unauthorized according to an access-control policy~\cite{DBLP:conf/osdi/ZhangSCPSS22,DBLP:conf/hotos/MarzoevASYKMKM19,DBLP:conf/uss/MehtaEH0D17,DBLP:conf/vldb/LeFevreAERXD04 .}}
elements.sent-2411-038: This process was extremely tedious and time-consuming.
elements.sent-2411-039: We reviewed feature documentation, inspected database schemas, experimented with the applications using sample data, and read parts of their source code (there was too much to read all of it).
elements.sent-2411-040: But we suspect that for any complex-enough codebase with contributions from many people, any single developer would need to go through a similar (albeit lighter) process.
elements.sent-2411-041: Using this information and common sense, we formulated policies that we thought would allow the endpoints to function correctly while protecting sensitive data.
elements.sent-2411-042: Despite our best efforts, we often discovered errors in our drafts, including an omission that would have leaked sensitive data---one that we only later discovered by chance.
elements.sent-2411-043: We embarked on the policy-extraction effort to make policy-writing less burdensome and error-prone.
elements.sent-2411-044: After building \name, we applied it to the same two applications and compared the extracted policies to our original, handwritten ones.
elements.sent-2411-045: As we detail in \Cref{sec:eval:compare , this exercise uncovered even more errors in our handwritten policies, which turned out to be both overly restrictive in some places (blocking legitimate access we hadn't anticipated) and, more critically, overly permissive in others (allowing unauthorized access).}
elements.sent-2411-046: Having \name would certainly have helped us write a more accurate policy with less effort from the start.
elements.sent-2411-047: Additionally, while reviewing the extracted policy, we discovered a subtle bug in the application code, which we had introduced years earlier when adapting the application for access control.
elements.sent-2411-048: This bug, caused by a misuse of an external library, silently rendered an access check a no-op (\Cref{sec:eval:compare ).}
elements.sent-2411-049: This experience shows that policy extraction can uncover access-control bugs, even if the policy is not ultimately enforced.
elements.sent-2411-050: Before delving into how policy extraction works, we first describe how our access-control policies are specified.
elements.sent-2411-051: We target web applications that store data in a relational database; when a user visits a page, the application issues SQL queries on the user's behalf and uses the query results to render the page.
elements.sent-2411-052: In this context, a classic way to specify access-control policies is to use view definitions~\cite{DBLP:conf/icde/Motro89,DBLP:conf/dmdw/RosenthalS00,DBLP:conf/sigmod/RizviMSR04 , which are SQL SELECT statements---parameterized by session parameters like the current user ID---that define information in the database that a user is allowed to access.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Under a view-based policy, a query is allowed only if it can be fully answered using the views.
elements.sent-2411-054: This criterion extends to a program that (conditionally) issues multiple queries, by treating the program as ``one big query'' that returns the results of its constituent queries.
elements.sent-2411-055: These notions can be made precise based on query determinacy~\cite{Nash10:determinacy ; but in the interest of space, we omit the formal definitions and instead offer an example.}

elements.sent-2411-112: This section explains how concolic execution works and why we chose it as the exploration strategy, and provides details about our tailored implementation.
elements.sent-2411-113: But we begin with some empirically-observed characteristics of typical web applications that motivated our choice of concolic execution.
elements.sent-2411-114: Given a web application's codebase, consider all program statements that issue SQL queries.
elements.sent-2411-115: Informally, imagine the backward program slice~\cite{DBLP:journals/tse/Weiser84 from these statements.}
elements.sent-2411-116: This slice, which we will call the query-issuing core, is what policy extraction is concerned with: it includes any program component on which a query-issuing statement has a control- or data-dependence, and omits the rest (e.g., HTML generation).
elements.sent-2411-117: While the entire codebase can be complex, it has been observed~\cite{DBLP:conf/osdi/Chlipala10,DBLP:conf/pldi/0001R19 that the query-issuing core of a typical web application is often simple.}
elements.sent-2411-118: We confirm this observation---for the applications we studied, the core consists mostly of:.
elements.sent-2411-119: Conditionals that test if a query's result set is empty~\cite[\S~2]{DBLP:conf/pldi/0001R19 or check basic conditions on primitive values (e.g., checking for equality or null)~\cite[\S~6.2]{DBLP:journals/toplas/ShenR21 ;.}}
elements.sent-2411-120: Loops over a query's result set~\cite[\S~2]{DBLP:conf/pldi/0001R19 with no loop-carried dependencies~\cite[\S~4]{DBLP:conf/osdi/Chlipala10 ; and,.}}
elements.sent-2411-121: Trivial data-flow to query statements (e.g., passing a value returned by one query to another).
elements.sent-2411-122: The third observation is possible because the applications issues parameterized SQL queries, so that no query-string formatting operation appears in the data flow.
elements.sent-2411-123: Our goal is then to analyze this simple query-issuing core while not wasting resources exploring the rest of the (more complex) codebase, with the additional challenge that the two parts of the code are not explicitly separated.
elements.sent-2411-124: We found concolic execution to be a technique that is up to this task.
elements.sent-2411-125: In concolic execution~\cite{DBLP:conf/pldi/GodefroidKS05,DBLP:conf/sigsoft/SenMA05 , a program is executed repeatedly using concrete inputs that have symbolic variables attached.}
elements.sent-2411-126: As the program runs, its state is tracked both concretely and symbolically.
elements.sent-2411-127: When the program branches on a symbolic condition, the condition and its outcome are recorded.
elements.sent-2411-128: This produces a conjunction of constraints (the path conditions) that led execution down a path.
elements.sent-2411-129: The conjuncts are then negated using a solver to generate new inputs (up to a bound) that will steer execution down other paths.
elements.sent-2411-130: We chose concolic execution because it offers a ``pay-as-you-go'' model for symbolic tracking: we selectively instrument only the operations that might appear in the query-issuing core, and the rest will simply execute concretely by default.
elements.sent-2411-131: This strategy not only reduces our instrumentation effort, but also mitigates path explosion---a branch on an uninstrumented condition will not cause a new path to be explored.
elements.sent-2411-132: Concolic execution has the additional benefit (like symbolic execution) of having ``no false positives'': Each path explored comes with a concrete input, which the user can use to recreate the run that led to a query being issued~(\Cref{sec:implementation:tool ).}
elements.sent-2411-133: We adopt the standard architecture of a driver responsible for generating inputs, and a set of executors.
elements.sent-2411-134: The driver maintains the set of explored paths in a prefix tree; for every prefix, it negates the last condition and invokes the Z3 solver~\cite{demoura08:z3 to generate a new input.}
elements.sent-2411-135: Our implementation keeps the prefix-tree in memory and generates inputs sequentially, but both can be relaxed to improve performance.
elements.sent-2411-136: The driver then sends the input to an executor, which executes a handler using an instrumented Ruby interpreter and the Ruby on Rails framework~(\Cref{sec:concolic:instrumentation ), and sends back a transcript, which captures path conditions and any queries issued (\Cref{sec:concolic:modeling ).}}
elements.sent-2411-137: The driver can dispatch inputs to executors running in parallel either locally or on a remote machine.
elements.sent-2411-138: Exploration terminates when all prefixes have been visited.
elements.sent-2411-139: Concolic execution requires symbolically modeling the handler's inputs, consisting of the database and session/request parameters.
elements.sent-2411-140: To ensure termination, the input space must be bounded.
elements.sent-2411-141: Following prior work~\cite{DBLP:conf/ccs/ChaudhuriF10 , we model the database as tables containing a bounded number of symbolic rows (our prototype uses a bound of~2).}
elements.sent-2411-142: Inspired by UrFlow's loop analysis~\cite[\S~4.2]{DBLP:conf/osdi/Chlipala10 , we also restrict the input space so that each query returns at most one row.}
elements.sent-2411-143: For simplicity, we will describe our algorithms under this assumption, even though they can be extended to handle queries returning multiple rows.

elements.sent-2411-170: As a first step, \name processes the transcripts for each handler into a set of conditioned queries.
elements.sent-2411-171: A conditioned query is a tuple $\langle sql, params, conditions \rangle$, where conditions is a list of prior Query and Branch records.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Simplifying a set of conditioned queries (\Cref{sec:policy:simplify}).}\label{alg:simplify} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \ForAll{conditioned query} \State remove vacuous branches\label{alg:simplify:remove-vacuous-br} \State propagate equalities\label{alg:simplify:propagate-eq} \State remove duplicate queries\label{alg:simplify:remove-dup} \EndFor \Repeat \ForAll{conditioned query}\label{alg:simplify:vacuous-loop} \State remove vacuous-and-unused query records\label{alg:simplify:remove-vacuous-qr} \EndFor \State \algorithmicrepeat~merge branches \algorithmicuntil~convergence\label{alg:simplify:merge-branches} \Until{convergence}\label{alg:simplify:outer-loop} \State remove subsumed\label{alg:simplify:remove-subsumed} \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-186: View generation is an iterative algorithm that walks down the list of conditions and ``conjoins'' each record onto an accumulated query definition~$A$.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{View generation from conditioned query (\Cref{sec:policy:generate-sql}).}\label{alg:generate-sql-view} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Procedure{GenerateSqlView}{cq} \State $A \gets \{\langle\rangle\}$ \Comment{Constant query returning empty tuple} \State $\mathcal{M}\gets \{\}$ \Comment{Maps query result column to $A$'s column} \ForAll{$cond \in cq.conds$} \If{$cond$ is $Branch(\theta, outcome=true)$} \State $A \gets \sigma_{\theta[\mathcal{M}]}(A)$ \ElsIf{$cond$ is $Branch(\theta, outcome=false)$} \State $A \gets \sigma_{\lnot \theta[\mathcal{M}]}(A)$ \ElsIf{$cond$ is $Query_{\ell}(sql, params)$} \State $Q_{\ell} \gets SqlToRa(sql, params)$ \State \Comment{Converts SQL query to relational algebra} \State $(A,\mathcal{M})\gets ConjoinQuery(Q_{\ell}, A, \mathcal{M})$ \EndIf \EndFor \State $Q_{\ell+1}\gets SqlToRa(cq.sql, cq.params)$ \State $(A,\mathcal{M})\gets ConjoinQuery(Q_{\ell+1}, A, \mathcal{M})$ \State return $RaToSql(A)$ \EndProcedure \Procedure{ConjoinQuery}{$Q_{\ell}, A, \mathcal{M}$} \State let $Q_{\ell}=\pi_{j_1,\ldots,j_m} \sigma_{\theta} (S_1\times S_2\times \cdots)$\Comment{Normal form} \State $n\gets \arity(A)$ \Comment{Number of columns in $A$} \State $\theta'\gets \theta[k\mapsto k+n][\mathcal{M}]$ \Comment{$\forall$~column index $k$} \State $A\gets \pi_{1,\ldots,n,n+j_1,\ldots,n+j_m}\sigma_{\theta'}(A\times S_1\times S_2\times \cdots)$ \State $\mathcal{M}\gets \mathcal{M}\cup\{ r_{\ell}.i \mapsto i + n \mid 1\leq i\leq m \}$ \State return $(A, \mathcal{M})$ \EndProcedure \end{algorithmic}

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{View generation from conditioned query (\Cref{sec:policy:generate-sql}).}\label{alg:generate-sql-view} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Procedure{GenerateSqlView}{cq} \State $A \gets \{\tupL\tupR\}$ \Comment{Constant query returning empty tuple} \State $\mathcal{M}\gets \{\}$ \Comment{Maps query result column to $A$'s column} \ForAll{$cond \in cq.conds$} \If{$cond$ is $Branch(\theta, outcome=true)$} \State $A \gets \sigma_{\theta[\mathcal{M}]}(A)$ \ElsIf{$cond$ is $Branch(\theta, outcome=false)$} \State $A \gets \sigma_{\lnot \theta[\mathcal{M}]}(A)$ \ElsIf{$cond$ is $Query_{\ell}(sql, params)$} \State $Q_{\ell} \gets SqlToRa(sql, params)$ \State \Comment{Converts SQL query to relational algebra} \State $(A,\mathcal{M})\gets ConjoinQuery(Q_{\ell}, A, \mathcal{M})$ \EndIf \EndFor \State $Q_{\ell+1}\gets SqlToRa(cq.sql, cq.params)$ \State $(A,\mathcal{M})\gets ConjoinQuery(Q_{\ell+1}, A, \mathcal{M})$ \State return $RaToSql(A)$ \EndProcedure \Procedure{ConjoinQuery}{$Q_{\ell}, A, \mathcal{M}$} \State let $Q_{\ell}=\pi_{j_1,\ldots,j_m} \sigma_{\theta} (S_1\times S_2\times \cdots)$\Comment{Normal form} \State $n\gets \arity(A)$ \Comment{Number of columns in $A$} \State $\theta'\gets \theta[k\mapsto k+n][\mathcal{M}]$ \Comment{$\forall$~column index $k$} \State $A\gets \pi_{1,\ldots,n,n+j_1,\ldots,n+j_m}\sigma_{\theta'}(A\times S_1\times S_2\times \cdots)$ \State $\mathcal{M}\gets \mathcal{M}\cup\{ r_{\ell}.i \mapsto i + n \mid 1\leq i\leq m \}$ \State return $(A, \mathcal{M})$ \EndProcedure \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-601: Although SQL is a familiar language, policies expressed as SQL views can be verbose and hard to comprehend given a complex application.
elements.sent-2411-602: We plan to investigate whether we can aid policy comprehension by translating them into natural language using large language models, by lifting SQL view definitions into a more succinct domain-specific language, or through visualization.
